subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 1! In this unit you will become an expert on all the numbers from 0 to 100. You use these numbers every single day, even when you do not notice. When Amina counts the goats coming home in the evening, when Yusuf checks that he has 64 pages left in his reading book, or when Halima counts out shillings at the Hargeisa market, they are all using numbers to 100.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Numbers are the language of the world around you. Traders in the market use them to give the right change. Farmers use them to count livestock and know if any goat is missing. Families use them to share food fairly at iftar during Ramadan. Even the prayer times follow numbers. When you become confident with numbers to 100, you are gaining a skill you will use every day for the rest of your life – and you are building the strong foundation you need for adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing in the units to come.

As you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the pictures, write the numbers, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas.
Read, write and represent numbers from 0 to 100.
Say and show the value of each digit in a two-digit number (tens and ones).
Count on and back in ones, twos and tens from any number.
Estimate how many objects there are, then count carefully to check.
Compare two numbers and use the symbols >, < and =.
Put a set of numbers in order from smallest to greatest.
Make and describe number sequences and use ordinal numbers (1st, 2nd, 3rd).

concepts.title: The 100 Square and Its Patterns
concepts.explanation: The 100 square is a grid that holds every number from 1 to 100. It has 10 rows and 10 columns, so there are 10 numbers in each row and 10 rows going down. Picture it like the shelves in Omar’s shop, with ten jars on every shelf and ten shelves altogether – one hundred jars in all. Look carefully and you will spot patterns that make numbers much easier to understand:
concepts.example: Every row starts with a number ending in 1 (1, 11, 21, 31 …).

concepts.title: Place Value – Tens and Ones
concepts.explanation: This is the big idea of the whole unit. Every two-digit number is built from tens and ones. The digit on the left tells you how many tens. The digit on the right tells you how many ones. Let us look closely at the number 47:
concepts.example: The 4 is in the tens place, so it stands for 4 tens = 40.

concepts.title: Different Ways to Show a Number
concepts.explanation: One number can wear many different clothes. Take the number 36. We can show it as: As a picture: | | | • • • • • • (3 sticks and 6 dots)
concepts.example: On a number line: a point between 30 and 40, closer to 40

concepts.title: Counting in Ones, Twos and Tens
concepts.explanation: Counting one at a time is fine for small groups, but slow for large ones. Imagine Idris counting 60 dates one by one – he would soon lose his place. Smarter counting saves time. Counting in ones: 41, 42, 43, 44, 45 … The ones digit changes each time; the tens digit stays the same until you pass a ten.
concepts.example: Counting in twos: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 … These are the even numbers. The numbers you skip (1, 3, 5, 7, 9) are the odd numbers.

concepts.title: Estimating Then Counting
concepts.explanation: An estimate is a sensible guess made before you count carefully. It is not a wild number pulled from the air – it is a thoughtful guess based on what you can see. Estimating first is a habit good mathematicians keep for the rest of their lives, because it warns you if your final count goes badly wrong. Suppose Omar tips a bag of dates onto a mat. Before counting, he glances and thinks, ‘That looks like about 30.’ Then he groups them into rings of ten and counts: 10, 20, 30, and 3 left over – 33 dates. His estimate…
concepts.example: How do you make a good estimate? Look at a small group you can count quickly – say ten objects – and picture how many of those groups would fill the whole pile. If the pile looks like about four of those groups,…

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Numbers
concepts.explanation: To compare two numbers means to decide which is greater, which is smaller, or whether they are equal. The trick is to use place value: First look at the tens digit. The number with more tens is greater.
concepts.example: If the tens are the same, then look at the ones digit to decide.

methods.title: How to compare two numbers
steps: Look at the tens digit of each number.
If one has more tens, that number is greater – stop.
If the tens are the same, compare the ones digits.
Write the correct symbol: >, < or =.

methods.title: How to count a large collection
methods.example: In the number 85, what is the value of the 8?
steps: Estimate first – make a sensible guess.
Group the objects into rings of ten.
Count the tens: 10, 20, 30 …
Count the leftover ones and add them on.

methods.title: Find the rule: how does each number change?
methods.example: Sagal has a basket of mangoes. She makes rings of ten and has 4 full rings and 6 left over. How many mangoes?
steps: Check the rule works between two or three pairs.
Apply the rule again and again to fill the gaps.
Check the find the rule: how does each number change? result carefully.

methods.title: Compare two numbers
steps: Solution: 75 has 7 tens; 57 has 5 tens.
Since 7 tens is more than 5 tens, 75 > 57.
The crocodile mouth opens towards 75.

methods.title: Continue a sequence
methods.example: A sequence starts at 37 and counts on in tens, stopping at 77. Write it out.
steps: Solution: Add ten each time: 37, 47, 57, 67, 77.
Notice the ones digit stays 7 the whole way, and the tens digit climbs by one each step.
Check the continue a sequence result carefully.

methods.title: Order a set of numbers
methods.example: Put these camel counts in order from smallest to greatest: 67, 42, 86, 34, 21.
steps: Solution: Compare the tens first.
The tens digits are 6, 4, 8, 3 and 2.
Order those from smallest: 21 (2 tens), 34 (3 tens), 42 (4 tens), 67 (6 tens), 86 (8 tens).
None of the tens are equal here, so we do not even need the ones.

workedExamples.title: Fill the gaps in a row
workedExamples.solution: Solution: This row counts on in ones. After 22 comes 23. After 24 comes 25. So the row is 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26. The ones digit goes up by one each step while the tens digit stays as 2.

workedExamples.title: Show the value of a digit
workedExamples.prompt: In the number 85, what is the value of the 8?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The 8 sits in the tens place, so it is worth 8 tens = 80. The 5 is worth 5 ones. So 85 = 80 + 5.

workedExamples.title: Count a collection the smart way
workedExamples.prompt: Sagal has a basket of mangoes. She makes rings of ten and has 4 full rings and 6 left over. How many mangoes?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 4 rings of ten = 40. Then add the 6 ones. 40 + 6 = 46 mangoes. Counting in tens first made this quick and safe.

workedExamples.title: Compare two numbers
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 75 has 7 tens; 57 has 5 tens. Since 7 tens is more than 5 tens, 75 > 57. The crocodile mouth opens towards 75.

workedExamples.title: Continue a sequence
workedExamples.prompt: A sequence starts at 37 and counts on in tens, stopping at 77. Write it out.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add ten each time: 37, 47, 57, 67, 77. Notice the ones digit stays 7 the whole way, and the tens digit climbs by one each step.

workedExamples.title: Order a set of numbers
workedExamples.prompt: Put these camel counts in order from smallest to greatest: 67, 42, 86, 34, 21.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Compare the tens first. The tens digits are 6, 4, 8, 3 and 2. Order those from smallest: 21 (2 tens), 34 (3 tens), 42 (4 tens), 67 (6 tens), 86 (8 tens). None of the tens are equal here, so we do not even need the ones. The order is 21, 34, 42, 67, 86.

workedExamples.title: An ordinal problem
workedExamples.prompt: Abdi is 4th in the line for water. The two people at the very front fill their buckets and leave. What position is Abdi in now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Two people left from the front, so everyone behind them moves up by two places. Abdi was 4th, so he becomes 4 − 2 = 2nd. If those two people had joined the back of the line instead, Abdi would still be 4th.

workedExamples.title: Make the smallest odd number
workedExamples.prompt: Using the digit cards 3, 5, 7 and 9, make the smallest possible odd two-digit number.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: To make a number small, put the smallest digit in the tens place, because tens count for the most. The smallest card is 3, so the tens digit is 3. To make it odd, the ones digit must be odd – and all the remaining cards (5, 7, 9) are odd, so choose the smallest of them, which is 5. The answer is 35.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Show 46 in three different ways (numeral, tens and ones, and a stick-and-dot picture).

workedExamples.title: What is the value of the underlined digit
workedExamples.prompt: What is the value of the underlined digit? a) 7 in 7͟3 b) 5 in 5͟8.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Using digit cards 2, 4, 6 and 8, make the largest possible even two-digit number and the smallest possible even two-digit number.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Largest even = 86; smallest even = 24 (0 is not among the cards, so use 2 in the tens and 4 in the ones).

activities.title: Make Your Own 100 Square
steps: Draw a grid with 10 boxes across and 10 boxes down.
Write the numbers 1 to 100, ten in each row.
Colour every number you say when counting in tens.
Colour the even numbers in a second colour. What pattern do you see?
Keep your 100 square – you can use it all through this unit as your number map.

activities.title: Bundles of Ten (Place-Value Sticks)
activities.materials: 47 small sticks, stones, or dry beans, and some string or rubber bands.
steps: Count out your objects and tie them into bundles of ten.
Count how many full bundles (tens) and how many are left over (ones).
Say the number: ‘___ tens and ___ ones make ___.’
Ask a family member to call out a number under 100 and build it with bundles and singles.

activities.title: The Crocodile Compare Game
activities.materials: paper number cards from 0 to 99 (write them yourself).
steps: Shuffle the cards and place them face down.
Each player turns over one card.
Draw a crocodile mouth (> or <) pointing at the greater number.
The player with the greater number keeps both cards. Play until the cards run out.
This game trains you to compare tens first, then ones – fast!

activities.title: Market Counting Investigation
activities.materials: a real or pretend market with items to count (dates, stones as ‘shillings’).
steps: Set out a pile of ‘goods’ and estimate how many there are.
Count them by making rings of ten, then counting in tens and ones.
Compare your estimate to the real count. How close were you?
Do it three times and see if your estimates get better.

activities.title: Ordinal Line-Up (Family Game)
steps: Line up five or more people.
Call out ordinals: ‘The 3rd person, hop!’ ‘The 1st person, clap!’
Now have someone leave the front. Ask everyone their new position.
Talk about how positions change when people join or leave.

activities.title: Number Sequence Snake
steps: Draw a long snake and divide its body into boxes.
Choose a starting number and a rule (for example, start at 20, count on in fives... or tens).
Fill the snake with your sequence.
Give a friend a snake with gaps and let them fill in the missing numbers.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand tens and ones best? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Place Value Rule
rules.text: Left digit = tens. Right digit = ones. 47 = 4 tens + 7 ones = 40 + 7.

rules.title: Comparing Rule
rules.text: Compare the tens first. More tens = greater number. If the tens are equal, compare the ones. The open mouth of > or < always points at the bigger number.

rules.title: Odd and Even Rule
rules.text: Even numbers end in 0, 2, 4, 6 or 8. Odd numbers end in 1, 3, 5, 7 or 9.

terms: What a digit is worth from its position
The two places in a two-digit number
commonMistakes: Say the tens first: five tens, one one → 51
The 4 is in the tens place, so it means 40
Open mouth faces the bigger number

realProblems.prompt: At Eastleigh market, Sumaya buys 3 bags of 10 sambusas and 5 loose sambusas for the family iftar. How many sambusas in total?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Liban counts 64 camels at the well. His cousin counts 46 at another well. Which well has more camels, and by how do you know?
realProblems.answer: Liban’s well (64 > 46, because 6 tens beats 4 tens).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Liban’s well (64 > 46, because 6 tens beats 4 tens).

realProblems.prompt: A shopkeeper in Kismaayo lines up prices: 25, 52, 35, 53, 33 shillings. Put them in order from cheapest to most expensive.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Noor collects water bottles in rings of ten. She has 7 full rings and 2 extra bottles. How many bottles altogether?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Aisha gives sweets to children who arrive. She counts them with ordinal numbers. If Ibrahim is the 9th child, how many children came before him?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

games.title: Quick Match: Digit
games.description: Practise digit through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: In the number 25, what is the value of the digit 5?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 5 is in the ones place, so it is worth 5.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: Counting on one from 96 gives 97.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Numbers are the language of the world around you. Traders in the market use them to give the right change. Farmers use them to count livestock and know if any goat is missing. Families use them to share food fairly at iftar during Ramadan. Even the prayer times follow numbers. When you become confident with numbers to 100, you are gaining a skill you will use every day for the rest of your life – and you are building the strong foundation you need for adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing in the units to come.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the pictures, write the numbers, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas.
I can read, write and represent numbers from 0 to 100.
I can say and show the value of each digit in a two-digit number (tens and ones).
I can count on and back in ones, twos and tens from any number.
I can estimate how many objects there are, then count carefully to check.
I can compare two numbers and use the symbols >, < and =.
